title: Seeds Before Sunset
synopsis: When Kofi discovers that his school's secret rooftop garden needs saving before sunset, he must gather friends and supplies to plant the seeds that will keep their special place alive. A heartfelt story about young environmental heroes working together to protect nature in the city.

pages.text:
The morning bell hadn't rung yet when Kofi climbed the rusty ladder to the roof. Below him, car horns honked and people rushed to work, but up here among the tomato plants and sunflowers, everything felt peaceful.

His teacher Ms. Chen was already there, her hands dirty with soil. She looked worried—more worried than Kofi had ever seen her.

"The landlord says the garden has to go," she said softly. "By sunset today, or they'll tear it all down tomorrow."

Kofi's heart sank. This garden was where his class had learned about seeds and growth. Where shy Emma had made her first friend. Where the butterflies came to rest during their long city journey.

illustration.description: Chalkboard style chalk drawing showing a boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes standing in a rooftop garden with raised beds full of vegetables and flowers. A woman in gardening clothes looks worried nearby. City buildings and morning light are sketched in white and colored chalk against the dark green board background. Mathematical equations about plant growth are doodled in the margins.
illustration.alt: Kofi and Ms. Chen in the worried rooftop garden

pages.text:
"But there must be something we can do," Kofi said. He always believed in solutions, even when problems seemed too big.

Ms. Chen pulled out a crumpled paper. "I made a list of what we'd need to convince them to let us stay. But it's a lot to do before sunset."

Kofi read the list:
- Prove the garden helps the neighborhood
- Show how many people care about it
- Get permission papers signed
- Plant the winter seeds we ordered

"The winter seeds are the most important," Ms. Chen explained. "If we plant them today, the garden will bloom all through the cold months. The landlord might see how beautiful it could be."

illustration.description: Chalkboard style illustration of a hand-drawn list on white chalk against dark green board. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes points to items on the list. School-themed doodles of equations and geometric shapes frame the scene, with dusty texture showing the artistic chalk work.
illustration.alt: Kofi looking at the garden rescue list

pages.text:
Back in their classroom, Kofi gathered his friends during morning work time. Emma, who loved drawing flowers. Marcus, who knew everything about bugs. Lily, who could organize anything.

"We need to save the rooftop garden," Kofi told them. "Today. Before sunset."

Dear reader, you should know that when Kofi set his mind on something important, his bright expressive eyes would shine with determination. His friends saw that shine now.

"What can we do?" Emma asked, already reaching for her art supplies.

Kofi showed them Ms. Chen's list, but in his head, he was making his own list—a list of worries:
- What if we can't get everyone to help?
- What if the seeds don't arrive in time?
- What if sunset comes too soon?

illustration.description: Chalkboard style chalk drawing of four children sitting around a school desk. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes speaks earnestly to three other students. School equations and formulas are doodled around the edges of the dark green board, creating a warm classroom atmosphere with white and colored chalk, dusty artistic texture.
illustration.alt: Kofi talking to his friends in the classroom

pages.text:
"I can make signs," Emma said right away. "Beautiful ones that show how important the garden is."

"I'll find everyone in our grade," Lily decided. "And maybe the older kids too."

Marcus nodded thoughtfully. "The garden has ladybugs and bees. They need those plants. I can explain that to people."

Kofi felt his worries getting smaller as his friends made their own lists of what they could do. Sometimes the biggest problems became smaller when you didn't carry them alone.

"Meet on the roof at lunch," he said. "We'll have just enough time before the sun gets low."

Through the window, Kofi could see the delivery truck pulling up to their school. His heart jumped. The winter seeds had arrived!

illustration.description: Chalkboard style illustration showing four children making plans together, their heads bent over papers and art supplies. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes points toward a window where a delivery truck is visible. White and colored chalk create the scene against dark green board, with school-themed mathematical doodles and warm, dusty artistic texture.
illustration.alt: The friends planning while the seed delivery arrives

pages.text:
By lunchtime, the rooftop buzzed with activity. Emma had made signs that read "Gardens Help Us Breathe" and "Home for City Butterflies." Lily had brought twelve other students. Marcus was showing everyone the tiny beneficial insects living among the plants.

But now Kofi had a new list forming in his mind—a list of things going wrong:
- The permission papers were still at the office
- Some of the winter seed packets had torn open
- The sun was already past the middle of the sky

Ms. Chen was on her phone, trying to reach the landlord. Her voice sounded strained as she explained about the children and the butterflies and the neighborhood cats who liked to sun themselves near the herbs.

illustration.description: Chalkboard style chalk drawing of a busy rooftop garden scene with many children working. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes kneels among scattered seeds while other students hold signs and tend plants. The sun is drawn in colored chalk, showing it's afternoon. City buildings and school equations are doodled around the edges against the dark green board with dusty texture.
illustration.alt: Busy rooftop garden with students working to save it

pages.text:
"Kofi," Emma called softly. "Look."

She pointed beyond the garden to the neighboring buildings. On several rooftops, people had come outside. A woman with a baby. An elderly man with binoculars. Two teenagers with a small dog.

They were all watching the children work in the garden.

"I think they care too," Emma whispered.

Kofi felt something shift inside him. Maybe their list of helpers was bigger than he'd thought. Maybe the garden mattered to more people than just their class.

"Let's wave," he said.

When they waved, every single person waved back. The baby clapped her tiny hands. The old man called out, "Keep going, kids!"

Suddenly Kofi's worry list didn't seem as important as his hope list.

illustration.description: Chalkboard style illustration showing children on a rooftop garden waving to neighbors on other rooftops and in windows. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes waves enthusiastically while Emma points to the people watching. White and colored chalk create the multi-level cityscape against dark green board, with mathematical equations doodled along building edges, dusty artistic texture throughout.
illustration.alt: Kofi and friends waving to supportive neighbors

pages.text:
The afternoon shadows grew longer. Kofi could see the sun starting to sink toward the buildings. His hands worked quickly, pressing winter seeds into small pots of dark soil.

Ms. Chen returned from her phone call, smiling for the first time all day. "The landlord is coming to see the garden for himself," she announced. "In one hour."

One hour. Kofi looked at the sun, then at their work. They'd planted half the seeds, but the garden still looked messy from all their hurrying.

"New list," he called to his friends. And this time, dear reader, it was a list of exactly what needed to happen:
- Finish planting the winter seeds
- Clean up the scattered soil
- Set up Emma's signs
- Make the garden beautiful

Everyone worked with quiet focus. Even the city noise below seemed softer, as if the whole neighborhood was holding its breath.

illustration.description: Chalkboard style chalk drawing showing children working intensely in the garden as shadows grow longer. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes plants seeds while the sun sits lower in the sky. Other students clean and organize around him. White and colored chalk show the urgent activity against dark green board with school-themed geometric doodles and dusty artistic texture.
illustration.alt: Children racing to finish the garden before sunset

pages.text:
Just as they placed the last seed in its pot, footsteps echoed on the ladder. A tall man in a business suit climbed onto the roof, followed by Ms. Chen.

The landlord looked around at the garden, at the children with dirty hands and hopeful faces, at the signs Emma had made. He walked slowly between the rows of plants, touching the tomato leaves, smelling the herbs.

"How long have you been growing this?" he asked.

"Two years," Ms. Chen said. "The children learn science here. They learn about taking care of living things."

The landlord nodded, then looked at the freshly planted winter seeds. "And these?"

"These will bloom all winter," Kofi said, his voice steady despite his racing heart. "For the butterflies who need places to rest. And for everyone who can see the roof from their windows."

The sun was touching the edge of the buildings now.

illustration.description: Chalkboard style illustration of a man in a business suit walking through the rooftop garden while children watch hopefully. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes stands among the planted seeds. The sun is very low on the horizon, drawn in warm colored chalk. School-themed equations and plant diagrams are doodled around the edges of the dark green chalkboard with dusty texture.
illustration.alt: The landlord examining the garden as sunset approaches

pages.text:
The landlord stood quietly for a long moment, watching the sun paint the garden golden. From the neighboring buildings, people watched too. The woman with the baby. The old man with binoculars. The teenagers with their dog.

"I hadn't realized how many people could see this garden," the landlord said slowly. "I hadn't thought about the butterflies."

He knelt down beside the winter seeds that Kofi had just planted. "These will really bloom in the cold?"

"Yes," Kofi said. "Ms. Chen taught us that some plants are strongest when things get difficult."

The landlord smiled—really smiled. "Then I think they should have the chance to grow."

As the sun touched the horizon, Kofi made one final list in his mind. But this time, it wasn't a list of worries or work. It was a list of what they'd saved:
- A place for butterflies to rest
- A classroom under the sky
- A garden that brings neighbors together
- Seeds that will bloom all winter long

illustration.description: Chalkboard style chalk drawing of the landlord kneeling next to planted seeds while children gather around. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes points to the seedlings. The sun sets in brilliant colored chalk behind city buildings, and neighbors can be seen watching from other rooftops. White and colored chalk create a warm, hopeful scene against dark green board with mathematical growth equations doodled in corners and dusty artistic texture.
illustration.alt: The landlord agreeing to keep the garden as the sun sets

pages.text:
Three months later, when snow covered the city, the rooftop garden bloomed with winter flowers—purple and white and bright yellow against the cold gray sky.

Kofi would climb up there sometimes before school, just to check on the plants they'd saved. The butterflies came, just as he'd promised. And on the neighboring rooftops, other people had started small gardens of their own.

Ms. Chen said the garden had planted seeds in more places than just the soil. It had planted ideas in people's hearts about taking care of growing things, about working together, about believing that children can save the world one small place at a time.

And you know what, dear reader? She was absolutely right.

The garden still grows there today, tended by new classes of children who make their own lists of hopes and dreams and ways to help their corner of the world bloom.

illustration.description: Chalkboard style chalk drawing showing the rooftop garden in winter, covered with snow but filled with blooming flowers in purple, white, and yellow chalk. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes tends the plants while butterflies flutter nearby. Other rooftops in the background show small gardens too. White and colored chalk create a magical winter scene against dark green board, with school equations about growth cycles doodled around the edges and warm dusty texture.
illustration.alt: The winter garden blooming with Kofi caring for it

metadata.lessonPlan: Discuss urban gardening, environmental stewardship, and how children can make a difference in their communities. Activities could include starting classroom seeds, making lists like Kofi's, or planning a school garden project.

coverPrompt.description: Chalkboard style illustration showing a lush rooftop garden with plants growing from containers and raised beds, the city skyline visible below. A boy with short cropped hair, rich dark brown skin, and bright expressive eyes kneels among seedlings. The sun is low on the horizon, casting warm chalk-drawn rays across the scene. White and colored chalk create the garden details against the dark green chalkboard background.
coverPrompt.alt: Cover showing Kofi in a rooftop garden at sunset

questions.question: Why did the garden need to be saved by sunset?
choices: The landlord would tear it down the next day
The children would forget about it
questions.explanation: Ms. Chen explained that the landlord said the garden had to go by sunset, or it would be torn down the next day.

questions.question: What made Kofi feel less worried about the big problem?
questions.explanation: When his friends made their own lists of what they could do, Kofi felt his worries getting smaller because he didn't have to carry the problem alone.

questions.question: Why were the winter seeds important to plant?
choices: They would bloom all through the cold months
questions.explanation: Ms. Chen explained that if they planted the winter seeds, the garden would bloom all through the cold months and show the landlord how beautiful it could be.

questions.question: Who else cared about the garden besides the students?
questions.explanation: When the children waved from the rooftop, people on neighboring buildings waved back, showing they cared about the garden too.

questions.question: What happened to other rooftops after the garden was saved?
choices: People started their own small gardens
questions.explanation: The story says that on neighboring rooftops, other people had started small gardens of their own, inspired by what the children had done.
